data.title: Microsoft: A Defensive Fortress In The Tariff Storm
data.description:
Market jitters are at an all-time high as the global economy braces for ongoing uncertainties in trade policy, particularly those related to the fickle state of U.S. reciprocal tariffs. The adverse implications of said policy shift, spanning increased import costs, global supply chain disruptions, and an economic downturn continue to loom large. And ensuing investor angst has accordingly prompted a reassessment of which stocks could demonstrate the most resilience amidst the wreckage.

Following months of moderating valuations in U.S. equities, made worse by tariff uncertainties in recent weeks, we believe Microsoft (MSFT) has emerged as a compelling long-term investment opportunity at current levels. The company’s software-centric, yet diverse business model also presents as a competitive advantage against its megacap tech peers, as it provides Microsoft with incremental resilience against impending tariff-driven vulnerabilities.

Inherently Low Direct Exposure to Tariffs

One of the most compelling strengths exhibited by Microsoft amidst the ongoing tariff standoff between the U.S. and the rest of the world is its minimal reliance on hardware. Given the reciprocal tariffs have been primarily levied on the import of goods, Microsoft’s software-centric business model provides it with significant mitigation against exposure to the ensuing cost and demand implications.

Specifically, Microsoft currently generates more than 90% of its revenue from software and services sales through its productivity and business processes (“PBP”) and intelligent cloud (“IC”) segments. Meanwhile, its slim hardware revenue mix generated from Surface and Xbox console sales, which are most prone to tariff cost implications, have already been on a consistent secular decline in recent years.

This represents a favourable sales mix for Microsoft amidst the tariff-heavy environment. Recall that the reciprocal tariffs announced to date have reached as high as 145% for China, with the situation still evolving. This is bound to squeeze margins for companies with significant reliance on global supply chains – a risk that Microsoft’s business model is inherently protected against.

Recession-Resistant Product Roadmap

Admittedly, no company can be entirely immune to impending macroeconomic uncertainties stemming from the aggressive tariff policy shift. Not only do tariffs raise the cost of goods, but the ensuing tightening of consumer spending could also trigger recessionary risks and unleash broader demand risks.

Specifically, economists are currently predicting a slower pace of 2025 U.S. GDP growth due to ongoing government spending cuts and tariff uncertainties, which risk triggering a recession. The average U.S. GDP growth estimate has decreased from +2.2% y/y earlier in the year to now +1.7% y/y. This represents a whopping 25% drop in the forward growth curve, which highlights the impending headwind facing corporate earnings. Although Microsoft operates under a software-centric business model, its business is bound to be indirectly exposed to tariff-driven cyclical headwinds, nonetheless.

Yet unlike its services-oriented megacap tech peers like Google (GOOG / GOOGL) and Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft’s differentiated by its sprawling portfolio of mission-critical offerings to both consumer and enterprise end-markets in a cyclical downturn. Despite its massive installed base, Microsoft continues to deliver strong double-digit percentage growth, thanks to persistent monetization improvements underpinned by its provision of essential solutions.

This is evident in the company’s recent disclosure of better-than-expected growth across both its Microsoft 365 Consumer and Commercial offerings. Specifically, Microsoft 365 Commercial Cloud revenue was a core driver of PBP segment growth during F2Q. The product generated revenue growth of 16% y/y, driven by strong uptake of E5 and Copilot add-ons. The company’s improving monetization of its commercial installed base was also evident in the fact that subscription revenue growth of 16% y/y outpaced seat growth of 7% y/y by almost 2x over the same period. This suggests resonation from enterprise end-markets with the value proposition of incremental productivity gains offered through new M365 offerings like Copilot and E5.

We expect these resilient monetization trends to persist ahead of the looming economic downturn, as optimization efforts resurface. This is already evident at tech companies like Shopify (SHOP) and Meta, which are increasingly touting ways to alleviate margins with the adoption of AI tools over incremental manual labour. Although the impending tariffs risk further curtailment in the hiring market, resilient monetization of Microsoft’s existing installed base via value-adding add-ons could offset moderation in seat growth.

Meanwhile, Microsoft 365 Consumer also delivered better-than-expected growth at 8% y/y during F2Q, despite an unfavourable mix shift to the lower-priced Basic tier. This suggests inelastic demand for Microsoft’s cloud-based productivity software in consumer end-markets, and limited impact to the product’s unit economics even amidst a downshift in consumer preference for lower-priced offerings. This also reinforces expectations for limited churn, despite the 30% price hike implemented in January for Copilot-equipped subscriptions (Basic tier pricing remains unchanged at $100/year).

In addition to Microsoft 365, Microsoft’s increasing cloud market share through Azure is also expected to mitigate its exposure to the impending tariff implications. Because of Azure’s comprehensive tech stack in supporting the ongoing AI transformation, it’s become deeply embedded in enterprise workflows. This is evident in ballooning demand for Azure compute capacity, increasing Microsoft Fabric and Power BI MAUs, and accelerating Azure AI Foundry run times disclosed by management during the F2Q earnings update. Businesses, regardless of economic conditions, are unlikely to cut back on these essentials, especially as compute capacity, data, and application development and deployment tools have become indispensable in the AI-first era. This accordingly generates sticky demand for Microsoft’s Azure revenue stream, which transcends economic cycles.

The anticipated resilience in Microsoft’s Azure revenues – especially those generated from AI services – is also corroborated by incremental compute capacity that’s coming online later this year. Recall that compute demand has been consistently outpacing supply at Microsoft since the onset of the AI sensation. But the consistently elevated level of capex deployed towards data center and server capacity over the past several quarters are expected to start coming online in the second half of FY25. This should improve monetization of pent-up demand in the coming quarters, which is supported by the significant acceleration in commercial bookings growth.

Admittedly, discretionary spending in both consumer and enterprise end-markets are poised to falter amidst renewed concerns that the elevated tariffs will reignite an inflationary environment. However, Microsoft’s diverse portfolio of mission-critical offerings, which are integral to enterprise operations, are expected to stay resistant against the emerging growth risks. This resilience in Microsoft’s core software and cloud computing offerings is especially significant amid the tariff disruptions, as it promises stability in uncertain times.

Unmatched Pricing Power

The mission-critical role of Microsoft’s product portfolio also yields unmatched pricing power even in the face of broader macroeconomic uncertainties. Businesses that have invested in Microsoft’s ecosystem – whether it’s the productivity software suite or Azure – are less likely to upend and leave given migration inefficiencies and ensuing egress charges.

This accordingly allows Microsoft to partially or even completely pass on any tariff-related costs to end customers in the form of price hikes. And the effectiveness of which is evident in Microsoft’s consistent pace of double-digit growth, despite its massive installed base and periodic price hikes across Microsoft 365, Office and Azure offerings over the years.

While its tech peers might find challenge in preserving margins due to their inherent reliance on global supply chains and subsequent exposure to tariffs, Microsoft’s software-centric business model and pricing power represents an invaluable advantage. This is further corroborated by Microsoft’s expectations for slight expansion in its FY25 operating margins, despite consistent acceleration in AI investments over the past year. The guidance implies stellar scalability, which will provide an incremental buffer against the increasing costs of growth that stem indirectly from tariffs.

Fundamental Considerations

As discussed in the earlier section, economists currently anticipate a 25% drop in NTM U.S. GDP growth estimates. We’ve considered this as a proxy for gauging the potential tariff-induced impacts to Microsoft’s earnings.

Our downside scenario forecast predicts FY26 revenue to grow at 10% y/y, which represents an approximate 25% discount to the 14% y/y growth anticipated in our previously discussed base case forecast. Despite ongoing market uncertainties, we don’t expect such a materially sustained drop in Microsoft’s forward growth curve. However, we believe the conservative assumption would be an adequate gauge for the worst-case scenario. This will be key to determining the price at which Microsoft has truly become an attractive opportunity. The lowered baseline revenue is expected to alter our prediction for Microsoft’s five-year CAGR to 9% in the downside scenario, versus 12% in the base case scenario.

Meanwhile, adjustments to Microsoft’s profit margins are immaterial in the downside scenario forecast, as the impending tariffs are unlikely to significantly disrupt the cost structure of its software-centric business model. This is further corroborated by Microsoft’s pricing power for its products, which will further help the company with preserving margins.

Valuation Considerations

Based on downside scenario fundamental projections, we expect Microsoft to present as a buying opportunity at any price level below $380 per share.

The downside scenario price target is computed under the discounted cash flow method. The cash flow projections considered are taken in conjunction with the downside scenario fundamental forecast discussed in the earlier section.

A 9.5% WACC in line with Microsoft’s risk profile and capital structure is applied to the analysis. The WACC calculation includes slight adjustments compared to the previously discussed base case assumptions to reflect changes in broader market and macroeconomic factors since (e.g., risk free rate, equity risk premium).

We’ve also applied a 3.5% perpetual growth rate on FY30E EBITDA in determining Microsoft’s terminal value. The 3.5% perpetual growth rate applied is in line with the higher range of long-term economic growth across Microsoft’s core operating regions. We believe the assumption is reflective of the pace in which Microsoft can grow sustainably into perpetuity at which it reaches steady-state in the long-run.

Conclusion

Although not entirely immune to impending macroeconomic risks, Microsoft’s relative stability amid ongoing tariff uncertainties makes the stock uniquely positioned against broader market volatility. In addition to its software-centric business model and resilient portfolio of mission-critical offerings, we believe Microsoft management is also taking a proactive stance in mitigating the company’s exposure to tariff headwinds.

Specifically, management’s repeatedly reiterated their promise to actively scale and adjust the allocation of capex spend based on “both near-term and long-term demand signals”. This was evident in Microsoft’s recent cancellation of certain data center leases, despite reiterating its $80 billion FY25 capex outlook. The undertaking suggests an earlier-than-expected shift from long-lived assets to “short-lived assets which are more correlated to revenue growth”. Coupled with incremental cloud computing capacity coming online in the coming quarters, which underpins Azure reacceleration, we believe Microsoft’s growth outlook remains intact and resistant against looming macroeconomic uncertainties.

As tariffs threaten to reshape global trade and upend forward economic growth prospects, Microsoft’s ability to adapt, innovate and deliver essential services to both consumer and enterprise end-markets make it a compelling defensive play. With the stock now also trading near our downside scenario price target, we believe Microsoft has presented itself as an opportunity amidst the broader market wreckage.

data.title: Seagate Technology Q3 Preview: Tariff Uncertainty Keeps Me Hesitant For Now
data.description:
Introduction

Seagate Technology Holdings plc (NASDAQ:STX) is due to report earnings on the 22nd of April, so I wanted to go through some numbers as well as what I am looking out for from the conference call in terms of updated comments on the company’s outlook, now that tariffs are the talk of the town.

What To Expect

Analysts expect the company to make $1.75 of adjusted EPS on $2.14B in sales. Sequentially, the top line declined by 8%, and a decent 29% growth y/y. How do these numbers stack up against what the management guided? For the upcoming quarter, the management expects the company will do around $2.1B in sales, plus or minus $150m, which is in line with the consensus midpoint value. Adjusted EPS to be in the range of $1.70, plus or minus $0.20, which is slightly below consensus estimates at the midpoint. Now, how likely is the company will beat or at least meet these estimates? As you can see below, the company did better at beating EPS estimates and much worse at sales.

It is also worth noting that there have been 12 EPS revisions to the downside as well as 4 revisions to the upside in the last 90 days. So, it is hard to tell what is going to happen.

What I’m Looking For

In terms of the company’s top-line growth, we can see that the demand for its products is alive and well. A 29% growth supports this view, so what is going to help it maintain such growth going forward? HDD revenues are the most promising growth for the company, with an impressive projected growth of 45% for 2025, driven by rising demand from major cloud providers due to continual AI advancements.

Cloud providers are driving HDD demand. All these enterprises require mass-capacity storage due to AI. The company’s Lyve Cloud platform is positioned very well to benefit from this trend going forward. What I’ll be looking out for is what these enterprises are going to do in terms of capex in AI spending. Just a couple of quarters ago, the big tech said they were going to spend a lot more this year on AI capex than they did in 2024, however, that was before all the tariff uncertainty. Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) will be reporting at the end of this month, and who knows if there will be any updates in the capex outlook due to tariff uncertainty. It would hurt many companies like STX if they were looking to reduce spending in this environment.

MSFT already paused its 2 gigawatts of US/ EU data-center projects due to oversupply and tariff uncertainty. Investors are already showing skepticism towards these companies as concerns about the returns from AI spending are worth it in the end, especially now that DeepSeek, from Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (DEEPSEEK), came in with a much lower cost model. So, it will be interesting to hear what the management has to say about the strength in the demand from these companies going forward.

In terms of the operational outlook for 2025, the company recently attended the Morgan Stanley Conference, in which CFO Gianluca Romano said that Seagate’s Heat-Assisted Magnetic Recording or HAMR technology, which enables 36TB drives, is ramping up with a major cloud provider, and is expected to go into full-scale production in late 2025. This is meant to boost exabyte output on the same unit capacity. Additionally, the company expects further operational efficiency by shifting to MR, or Magnetoresistive, technology, which will improve sensitivity and efficiency in HDDs, enabling higher capacities and a much better performance. This is also projected to boost the company’s sequential revenue growth this year.

Now, all this is great to hear. However, what is going to happen in reality may be quite different if the Trump Administration is going to go ahead and implement some sort of tariffs in the future. Over the weekend, President Trump put exemptions on a lot of consumer electronics including HDDs from the 125% tariffs on Chinese imports. This will likely help the company tame its costs for now. However, given the flip-flop nature of the “art of the deal,” I am not fully certain that this is the end of tariff wars right now, and I wouldn’t be surprised if Trump goes ahead and scares the markets once again with another round of sweeping tariffs. The original tariffs on Chinese imports are still in effect, so I expect to hear something regarding increased costs in the upcoming earnings call.

Closing Comments

Seagate Technology Holdings plc does have a decent growth runway going forward, and I expect it to perform well in the future. However, because of this uncertainty regarding the ripple effects of tariffs still to come and the ones that are already in effect, I am hesitant to give it a buy rating for now.

I am going to tune in to the company’s conference call as well as the big tech earnings calls to hear what they have to say regarding their capex spending in 2025 and whether the idea has changed since tariffs became the prominent talking point around the world.

Seagate’s share price has dropped considerably since January and is becoming a lot more attractive. Thus, I will revisit the company in a few months after we hopefully get a lot more certainty regarding the economic outlook and how these tariffs going to affect companies like Seagate.

data.title: Comcast: Something Epic Is Coming
data.description:
I don't know about you, but I have always loved theme parks. Some of my greatest memories have been the couple of times that I have visited theme parks owned by The Walt Disney Company (DIS), with examples being the Magic Kingdom, Disney’s Animal Kingdom, Disney’s Hollywood Studios, and Epcot, with the last of these being my personal favorite. However, next month, on May 22nd, something "epic" is coming onto the scene that risks Disney's position as the go-to destination in Florida. That is none other than Epic Universe, which is a new theme park being opened at Universal Orlando Resort, which is owned by NBCUniversal, a subsidiary of entertainment giant Comcast Corporation (NASDAQ:CMCSA).

This multi-year initiative will be the first theme park opened in Florida in 26 years. We don't know the exact cost of it, but third party estimates have put a price tag on it of $7.7 billion. That covers 750 acres of space. To put that in perspective, even Disney’s Animal Kingdom, which is the largest of the parks at Walt Disney World, occupies only 580 acres. Even if you added up Epcot, Magic Kingdom, and Disney’s Hollywood Studios together, you would get a size of only 547 acres.

This is a big gamble being made by Comcast. But all the data currently available suggests to me that it's likely to be a big winner. We won't know for at least a year or two exactly how successful this theme park will be. Furthermore, there is uncertainty because of broader economic concerns that could harm the U.S. economy in the near term. But when you add on top of this how cheap shares of Comcast currently are, I would argue that the company makes for a clear "strong buy" candidate at this point in time.

Years in the making

Epic Universe is an initiative that has been years in the making. Originally, construction on it began in August of 2019. But because of the COVID-19 pandemic, work was halted in July of 2020 and wasn't resumed until March of 2021. With COVID-19 now a distant memory, and the economy having recovered significantly, the theme park is getting close to launch. In fact, the management team at Comcast has said that opening day will be May 22nd of this year. That's a little more than a month from the present moment.

Inclusive of this major investment by the company, Comcast has invested over $10 billion in Universal destinations projects from 2018 through 2024. This is if we include its investments in California, Texas, and Nevada as well. Management describes Epic Universe as a technologically advanced theme park that will operate as a,

"weeklong, four-theme-park vacation destination featuring three theme parks, one water park, CityWalk dining retail, and entertainment district, and 11 hotels."

Most of the hotels, to be fair, were built outside of this initiative. Only three of them are new and related to Epic Universe.

The first of its themed worlds is called The Wizarding World of Harry Potter – Ministry of Magic. Although JK Rowling, the author of the series, has become a very controversial figure in recent years, the magic is still alive with the Harry Potter brand. This is evidenced not only by this theme park, but also by spin-off films in recent years and the fact that Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) will begin filming an entire Harry Potter television series this summer.

The second world included in Epic Universe is appropriately called Super Nintendo World and will feature different attractions like being able to challenge Bowser on the park’s Mario Kart ride, venturing into Donkey Kong Country, and more. Another world called How to Train Your Dragon – Isle of Berk will focus on the DreamWorks Animations hit movies and will bring with it a Viking themed village that includes Dragons and other attractions. Epic Universe will also include Dark Universe, a theme park dedicated to horror movie monsters such as Frankenstein. On top of all of this, the company will also have a ride, entertainment, dining, and shopping district by the name of Celestial Park.

Management expects Epic Universe to have a major positive impact, not only on shareholders, but on the local economy. For instance, the company claims already that it's direct investment into the theme park has had an $11 billion economic impact across the U.S. In the first full year of operation, the park is expected to generate around $2 billion for the state of Florida. After its first year of operations, it anticipates generating $386 million in state and local taxes, $120 million in property taxes, and $500 million in sales taxes for the state. They expect a $13 million tax inflow associated with tourist development taxes, and the firm is investing $230 million into improving public facilities as part of this. Ultimately, the company expects to create 17,500 new jobs nationwide in the first full year that this project is in effect. Around 10,290 of those jobs are likely to occur within Epic Universe itself, with the remainder being off site. This is all on top of the 65,000 temporary jobs created as a result of the park’s construction.

We have no idea how much revenue will be generated from this initiative. However, the sales tax estimates provided by management give us a clue. A good estimate for the blended sales tax rate in Florida is 6.5%. This would imply annual revenue, starting after the first year of operations, of $7.69 billion. To put this in perspective, in 2024, Comcast’s theme parks alone ended up generating $8.62 billion in sales. That was up from the $7.54 billion reported two years earlier.

The company has come to understand over the years that theme parks are a major source of not only revenue, but also cash flow. Take Disney as an example. If we look at just the Experiences operations of the entertainment behemoth, revenue from 2022 to 2024 rose from $28.09 billion to $34.15 billion. Over this window of time, theme park admissions revenue on its own grew from $8.60 billion to $11.17 billion. But of course, the revenue includes other things as well, such as resorts and vacations, licensing out content, and more.

Even though Experiences accounted for 36.7% of the revenue that Disney generated in 2024, the operations in it were responsible for 59.4% of the firm's total segment profits. Unfortunately, there are significant comparability issues when it comes to how Comcast reports its theme park data and how Disney reports its data. Comcast is much more obscure with its results. However, to the best that I can, I was able to show below the segment EBITDA and EBITDA margins generated by Disney’s Experiences business and how those figures stand up against what Comcast achieves.

These numbers are within a fairly narrow range of one another. So already, Comcast understands how profitable the theme park business can be. Doubling down on it, literally, makes a great deal of sense. At the end of the day, Epic Universe is certainly the largest initiative being worked on by Comcast at this time. But it's not the only destination concept it is putting its efforts toward. In Frisco, Texas, the company is building the first-ever Universal Kids Resort, which is a family theme park concept that will include an integrated 300 room hotel. This would include featured lands themed around DreamWorks Animations characters such as Shrek, Puss in Boots, Trolls, and Gabby’s Dollhouse. It will also include Nickelodeon’s SpongeBob SquarePants, Illumination’s Minions, and even Jurassic World. This is currently expected to open in 2026. In Hollywood, the company is working on a new rollercoaster dedicated to the Fast & Furious franchise, plus it opened a DreamWorks Land in Orlando in 2024.

It's truly unclear at this time what will happen consider the economy. It wouldn't be shocking to see traction struggle after an initial spike in traffic. However, in looking at ticket pricing, I regularly ran into the message that volumes on the company's website were too high. An image of that experience can be seen above. What I do know is that, over the long haul, Florida has been a major destination for tourists. In 2022, the state saw 137.4 million tourists come in. By 2024, that number had grown to 142.9 million.

The vast majority of these are domestic tourists. But the company does receive over 10 million international tourists each year. We don't have data for 2024, but we do know that the out of state spending in 2023 associated with tourism for the state was $131 billion. This represents an increase over the $124.9 billion generated just one year earlier.

The state saw $8.5 billion in tax revenue associated with the tourism industry, with 84% of that being sales tax. Local governments saw another $8.6 billion in tax revenue from this activity. Not surprisingly, tourism is the second-largest source of employment in Florida with 1.39 million workers. This is only behind the health and social services category at 1.57 million. In 2023, the industry was responsible for supporting $76 billion in wages. That's an increase over the $73 billion seen one year earlier and represented the largest wage support in the state's history for that space.

Even if Epic Universe doesn't pan out in the way that investors hope, there is no denying that Comcast is an attractive prospect. From 2023 to 2024, the company saw adjusted net income grow from $16.49 billion to $16.94 billion. Adjusted operating cash flow, which strips out changes in working capital, expanded from $27.49 billion to $32.62 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business expanded from $37.63 billion to $38.07 billion. In the chart below, you can see how cheap shares are on an absolute basis. These are incredibly low multiples as I see it.

Takeaway

At the end of the day, I believe that Epic Universe makes for an interesting opportunity for Comcast. Even as things stand with the company at the moment, shares are attractively priced. Recent financial performance is encouraging and, while we could see weakness in the near term, I see no reason why the long-term outlook wouldn't be positive given the tourism numbers that we have seen for Florida. Add all of this together, and I do believe that Comcast makes for a very solid "strong buy" candidate. And I'm not the only one. Management even recently announced a new $15 billion share buyback program. Given how cheap the stock is, this seems to be a no-brainer to me.

data.title: Dreamland Limited Seeks U.S. IPO On High Valuation And Risks
data.description:
Dreamland Is Growing But Valuation And Risks Are High

Dreamland Limited (TDIC) has filed to raise $9 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to an SEC F-1 registration statement.

TDIC provides event management services in the greater Hong Kong region.

The company is growing revenue from a tiny base, but is thinly capitalized and is seeking wildly excessive valuation multiples at IPO.

My outlook on the TDIC IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Dreamland Do?

Hong Kong, PRC-based Dreamland Limited was founded to assist event organizers in their themed touring walk-through experience events in Hong Kong.

Management is headed by founder, and CEO Ms. Seto Wai Yue, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2016 and was previously the founder of The Stairs, a luxury retail store and has experience in the development and management of a well-known cartoon character.

The company’s primary offerings include the following:

  • Event Organizing.

  • Planning.

  • Promoting.

  • Managing.

  • Merchandise Design.

  • Store Management.

As of September 30, 2024, Dreamland has booked a fair market value investment of $261,300 from investors, including the founder.

The company has more than eight years of operation and has completed over 30 large-scale events and provided related services to IP owners and event organizers of various sizes.

The firm counts more than 30 brand owners, 15 IP owners and event organizers as clients with their bases of operations in Hong Kong, Taiwan, Japan, and the US.

Regarding related party transactions, the company and its founder have extensive related party relationships and transaction history, as detailed here.

Market Overview For Dreamland

According to a 2025 market research report by Grand View Research, the Chinese MICE (Meetings, Incentives, Conferences, and Events) market was estimated at $81.2 billion in 2024 and is expected to exceed $139 billion by 2030.

This represents an expected CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) of a fairly robust 9.5% from 2025 to 2030.

The primary reasons for this expected growth are expected to be the Incentives segment, which is forecasted to produce the highest growth through 2030, although the Meetings segment was the largest revenue-generating segment in 2024.

Various competitive or other industry participants include the following:

  • BCD.

  • Maritz.

  • Conference Care.

  • ATPI Limited.

  • FCM Travel Solutions.

  • Global Cynergies.

  • ITL World.

  • Questex.

  • One10.

  • Creative Group.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and expected future growth trajectory of the Chinese MICE market from 2018 through 2030:

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Dreamland:

Strengths

  • Full suite of offerings, creative capabilities, and network of partnerships.

Weaknesses

  • Concentrated revenue, fixed price project risks, limited market penetration.

Opportunities

  • Digital growth plans, geographic expansion, moving up the value chain.

Threats

  • Intense and fragmented competition, regulatory unpredictability, IP licensing risks.

Dreamland’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

As of September 30, 2024, Dreamland had $177,210 in cash and $3.0 million in total liabilities.

IPO Details For Dreamland

Dreamland intends to raise $9 million in gross proceeds from an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, with the company offering 1.34 million shares at a proposed midpoint price of $4.50 per share.

The sole existing shareholder, the founder, has indicated that she intends to sell 660,000 shares in the IPO. This is a negative signal and is uncommon for the founder and CEO to do.

Even so, the founder will have a significant majority of voting control immediately after the IPO, also resulting in key-person risk and potential related transaction risks.

No outside investors have shown a desire to purchase block shares in the IPO.

With a completed IPO, the firm’s enterprise value would be approximately $135 million, not including the effects of underwriter over-allotment options.

The float to outstanding shares ratio (ex-underwriter over-allotments) will be approximately 6.45%, so the stock will be a ‘low float’ stock subject to potentially much higher volatility in open market trading.

Management said the dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $4.36, which is within the typical range of companies at IPO.

The company, directors, executive officers, and 5% or greater shareholders will be subject to the standard 180-day shareholder sale lock-up period.

Management disclosed it intends to apply the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

approximately 10% to acquire a multi-territorial IP license;

approximately 10% to set up our own ticketing platform;

approximately 15% for possible strategic acquisitions;

approximately 15% to expand our marketing department and financing and administration department;

approximately 10% for upgrading our enterprise resource planning system;

approximately 10% for repayment of loans made by Ms. Seto to us in connection with the payment of costs and expenses in connection with this offering and obtaining a listing of our Shares on the Nasdaq. The facility is unsecured, and interest-free in the aggregate amount of up to HK$12,000,000. As of the date of this prospectus, the amount drawn down is approximately HK$3.7 million and is repayable on the earlier of the completion of this offering and September 30, 2025; and

the balance for working capital and other corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC).

The company's online roadshow is not available.

Regarding outstanding legal proceedings, the leadership said the firm is not subject to any legal claims or arbitration that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or results from operations.

The only listed bookrunner of the IPO is Bancroft Capital, LLC.

Tiny Dreamland Is Growing But Risks And Valuation Are High

TDIC is seeking US public capital market funding for its various growth and expansion initiatives.

The firm’s financials have produced increasing topline revenue from a small base, growing gross profit but uneven gross margin, variable operating profit, and a swing to cash used in operations in the most recent reporting period.

Free cash flow during the twelve months ended September 30, 2024, was negative ($4,993).

The company's growth strategy involves increasing its vertical integration to capture a greater share of the customer’s wallet for each event, launching a ticketing platform, and securing wider IPO licensing rights for broader event opportunities.

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have varied greatly in recent years; its Selling, G&A efficiency multiple fell to 6.4x in the most recent reporting period, a negative signal.

The firm currently intends to pay according to a wide variety of factors that the Board of Directors will evaluate on a periodic basis. The company is subject to numerous restrictions on dividends and transfers of funds in the PRC and in the Cayman Islands.

TDIC hasn’t made capital expenditures in the trailing twelve-month period.

The market opportunity for providing MICE services in China is large and expected to grow at a reasonably robust rate of growth in the coming years after a sharp fall during the pandemic period.

The company will have risks as a public entity, including its ‘foreign private issuer’ and ‘emerging growth company’ status, which enable management to disclose substantially less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly in recent years.

Also, the newly confirmed head of the SEC, Paul Atkins, has indicated he intends to launch investigations into Chinese public companies and their reporting compliance or lack thereof.

This may significantly chill the market for new Chinese company issuers.

Some of the company's revenue comes in from countries in Southeast Asia denominated in local currencies and the firm does not engage in hedging strategies. So it is vulnerable to currency fluctuations and potential devaluation against the US dollar.

Management is seeking an EV/Revenue multiple of approximately 22.5x and an EV/EBITDA multiple of 81x, which indicates excessive valuation expectations for such a small company with numerous risks for investors.

Accordingly, my outlook on the TDIC IPO is to sell (avoid it).
