data.title: Taking A Pass On Midland States Bancorp
data.description:
In my search for interesting banks, one company that I came across that, I thought, was worth digging deeper into is Midland States Bancorp (NASDAQ:MSBI). With a market capitalization as of this writing of $342.2 million, the enterprise is certainly on the small side of the spectrum. These kinds of opportunities have a tendency to go overlooked by investors. The hope here is that these firms have the chance to deliver market-beating returns because the market is not paying attention to them. I have had the opportunity to find businesses like this in the past that have gone on to do very well for themselves and their investors. Unfortunately, I don't think that Midland States Bancorp will join those ranks.

Over the past few years now, the company has actually seen its balance sheet shrink. Revenue and profits have also fallen. It would be one thing if the company had high asset quality and was trading on the cheap. It is, admittedly, quite cheap on a price to book and price to tangible book basis. But in every other regard, the firm looks unappealing. This is disappointing because it actually has an interesting wealth management division that has grown its assets recently. But at the end of the day, this is not enough to offset the negatives that I'm looking at. Because of this, I have decided to rate the company a very solid ‘sell’. And that might be a bit generous.

Not banking on this name

According to the management team at Midland States Bancorp, the company operates as a diversified financial holding firm based out of Effingham, Illinois. It has bank branches located not only throughout Illinois, but also in parts of Missouri. From these locations, the enterprise provides customers with different commercial and consumer banking products and services. It offers up business equipment financing, merchant credit card services, trust and wealth management services, and both insurance and financial planning services as well.

In recent years, the enterprise has seen its balance sheet shrink. From 2022 through 2024, the value of deposits on its books has dropped from $6.36 billion to $6.20 billion. During times of weakness or during times when a bank is looking to grow rapidly, it's not uncommon for the business to take on brokered deposits. These are deposits that are borrowed from other financial institutions. Unfortunately, there is no guarantee these will be there for the long haul, though companies like this do lock them down for a specific period of time. However, it is particularly noteworthy to me that the deposit situation for the institution would have looked even worse had it not been for these brokered deposits. Back in 2022, it had $12.8 million worth on its books. By 2023, that number had grown to $94.5 million. And by 2024, it had ballooned to $259.5 million. Even though these still account for only a small portion of the firm's deposits, the trend there and the trend in deposits as a whole is discouraging at best.

When deposits start shrinking, other parts of the balance sheet typically start to shrink as well. That is exactly what we have seen over the last three years. From 2022 through 2024, the value of loans on the company's books contracted from $6.25 billion to $5.09 billion. The largest chunk of these loans, about $2.59 billion, ended up being dedicated to commercial real estate. Next in line was the $1.74 billion involving total commercial loans and leases, which was comprised mostly of $921.9 million of commercial loans. Equipment finance loans and equipment finance leases accounted for the majority of the rest, at $417 million and $391.4 million, respectively. The business also has around $299.8 million of construction and land development loans, $380.6 million of residential real estate loans, and $157.2 million of consumer loans.

There has been a notable increase in the value of securities on the books in recent years. From 2022 through 2023, these expanded from $776.9 million to $1.21 billion. But this is really the only significant portion of the asset side of the balance sheet that has gotten better. Cash and cash equivalents dropped from $160.6 million to $114.8 million. The good news is that we have seen debt decline. In 2022, it was $652.1 million. By 2024, it had fallen to $474.5 million.

Unfortunately, the company's balance sheet problems have ultimately caused problems with the income statement. From 2022 through 2024, net interest income plummeted from $225.6 million to $92.6 million. Some of this can be blamed on a drop in the company's net interest margin from 3.57% to 3.15%. This is thanks largely to the high-interest rate environment that we have dealt with over the last couple of years and the impact that it has on the willingness of depositors to put their money into low yielding accounts when other opportunities are superior. However, the biggest hit in 2024 came from the $129.3 million provision for credit losses that the company booked. Year over year for the final quarter, the business went from charging off 0.33% of its loans to charging off 7.23%. Total net charge offs amounted to $102.7 million in the final quarter of 2024 alone.

If there is one bright spot to the company is that it generates a lot of revenue from non-interest income activities. In 2024, for instance, it saw $28.7 million of revenue associated with its wealth management business. Total assets under administration during the final quarter of 2024 amounted to $4.15 billion. That's up nicely from the $3.73 billion reported the previous year.

The business has seen some growth recently when it comes to other aspects of its business. This includes income on company owned life insurance, residential mortgage banking revenue, and service charges on deposit accounts. But even with all this factored in, non-interest income actually dipped over the last three years from $79.9 million to $77.7 million. Because of these weak spots, Midland States Bancorp has gone from generating a net profit of $95.9 million in 2022 to generating a net loss of $22.3 million last year.

Unfortunately, this makes the business impossible to value relative to earnings. I even considered using management's adjusted earnings. But even those show a net loss for the year. You would have to completely ignore the provision for credit losses in order to get positive income for 2024. But we can value the company based on both its price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In the chart above, you can see how the business stacks up. It currently is cheaper than the five companies that I compared it to. But honestly, I would say there's a good reason for that.

Asset quality is also problematic. Because of the poor financial performance for 2024, the business has negative return on assets and negative return on equity. These can both be seen in the chart above and the chart below, respectively. Furthermore, because of these weak results, the enterprise is worse off than any of the five companies I compared it to.

Takeaway

As things stand, I cannot in good conscience be anything other than bearish about Midland States Bancorp. The company seems to have issues and while shares are cheap on a price to book and price to tangible book basis, I think that cheapness is justified because of its declining balance sheet, and its meaningful net loss in 2024. Unless something changes, I don't see this turning out well for shareholders. This has led me to rate the company a very solid ‘sell’. And in all honesty, it wouldn't take much for me to downgrade it to a ‘strong sell’.

data.title: A Warning To Baby Boomers Regarding The U.S.-China Trade War And Interest Rates
data.description:
The Thucydides Trap refers to the natural, inevitable disturbance that occurs when a rising power threatens to displace a ruling power. The “Trap” is war. For now, it’s a trade war. Because most of our 78 million baby boomers are currently in the Retirement Risk Zone, they are most exposed to the potential consequences, at this time in their lives.

China has the upper hand because it is less reliant on the US than the US is reliant on China. Prices of US bonds reflect the fear underlying this trade war. Intermediate-long term bonds have been bid up as investors move to safety.

Edge China

According to the Office of the United States Trade Representative and the Visual Capitalist, The following table summarizes US and China exports and imports.

The edge goes to China because only 6% of its imports are from the US, while 13% of US imports are from China. According to the Council on Foreign Relations, both importers and exporters can suffer from tariffs, but importers are often seen as bearing a greater burden, especially in the context of the US. Tariffs increase the cost of imported goods, impacting both consumers and businesses that rely on those imports. While exporters may initially try to absorb the tariff costs, they may also face reduced demand due to the higher price.

As shown in the following graph from Mapped: How China Overtook the U.S. in Global Trade (2000–2024), China has become dominant in world trade, so it is a formidable adversary. China is now the dominant trade partner for most of Asia, Eastern Europe, the Middle East, Oceania, South America, and Africa. In other words, China’s economy has plenty of alternative trade partners to the US.

Capital markets have taken “the Trap” seriously, with most attention focused on the US stock market, but bond prices have also been affected as they have been bid up by investors fleeing to safety.

Fear and greed in the US bond market

Be fearful when others are greedy. - Warren Buffett

The yield curve for US Treasuries is currently U-shaped, which is very rare. The press has reported that the temporary deferment of most tariffs is due to the reaction of the bond market rather than the stock market. Interest on our national debt could cripple the economy and lead to serious inflation.

Normal yield curves are upward sloping because investors demand a risk premium for taking on the volatility in long-term bonds. Let’s call this “greed” driven.

Sometimes the yield curve inverts to pay higher yield for short term, lower risk, T-bills. This unusual situation tends to precede recessions, so we’ll call this “fear” driven. Fear is currently driving up the prices of intermediate-long term bonds.

But the yield curve is very rarely both “normal” and “inverted” -- U-shaped. What is going on? One interpretation is that fear is imminent, causing demand for safety to swell near term, but greed sets in at very long maturities to compensate for the high risk.

It’s also worth noting that the Fed is allowing its long-term bond holdings to mature without replacement in order to reduce its balance sheet under quantitative tightening ('QT'). The Fed is no longer buying long-term bonds in order to manufacture a zero interest rate policy ('ZIRP').

Conclusion

These are scary times. No surprise, the typical advice is to stay the course – that it will all work out fine -- but those near retirement should take heed because they could find the rest of their lives undermined by Sequence of Return Risk – losses at this stage in life hurt the most. Most baby boomers are currently in the Retirement Risk Zone.

I’ve written in the past about why and how they should protect themselves. Please check out my warnings. For example, see Baby Boomers Better Get Out Of The Stock Market Now and When Stocks And Bonds Won't Do

What do you think? What is happening here? What will happen?

data.title: Why The Next Recession Could Be Steeper Than Usual
data.description:
Recession-Plagued Nation Demands New Bubble To Invest In

WASHINGTON—A panel of top business leaders testified before Congress about the worsening recession Monday, demanding the government provide Americans with a new irresponsible and largely illusory economic bubble in which to invest.

--The Onion, July 14, 2008

The above satirical news item was likely intended to lend a degree of levity to morose market conditions during the Great Recession of 2007-09. Several months after it was published, the article proved to be remarkably prophetic.

In mid-September of 2008, the overnight repo market seized up in a type of liquidity cardiac arrest that no one had ever witnessed, let alone seen coming. This led to a series of emergency measures known as quantitative easing ('QE'). So much money was created over four rounds of QE, this novel remedy may have laid the groundwork for a market super-bubble, though this was hardly the intention at the time.

The Overnight Repo Market; How It All Began

Reverse purchase agreements have been around since WWI. Originally, the Federal Reserve used this financial policy to provide temporary liquidity to big banks and other financial entities by way of large, short-term loans. At first, only the Federal Reserve provided this type of service to the banking sector. However, other banks quickly picked up on the practice and began providing funds to finance their own short-term, interbank loans. This became known as the ‘overnight repo market’.

These transactions generally play out as follows. Banks holding a surfeit of liquidity can earn interest on that extra cash by lending it to other banks via the overnight repo market. The lending bank agrees to buy a financial instrument from the borrowing bank—usually a bond collateralized by mortgage-backed securities ('MBS'). The lending bank buys the bond at a price slightly less than the bond’s face value. This is known as a “haircut” and is the equivalent of interest paid on a loan. The bond seller agrees to repurchase the bond on a specified date. This action effectively pays off the loan and closes the transaction.

The overnight repo market hummed along without a hitch until banks loosened their mortgage lending standards in the early 2000s under the false assumption that soaring real estate prices would continue indefinitely. Some analysts point the finger at the Community Reinvestment Act of 1977, which encouraged banks to make home loans to low-income borrowers. Others suggest the repeal of the Glass-Steagall Act, which led to a wide array of financial deregulation, was also responsible insofar it allowed for lower lending standards.

As banks fell into the habit of providing home loans to residential borrowers of questionable financial means, the MBS that collateralized most of the bonds sold in the overnight repo market became increasingly suspect.

Meanwhile, by December 2007 the economy had officially entered a recession. When the Fed applied its usual remedy of lowering the prime rate, it clearly had an effect on the benchmark 10-year Treasury. From September 2007 through April 2008, the Fed lowered rates seven times, dropping the Prime Rate from 4.75% to 2.00% for a total of 275 basis points ('bps'). While this action didn’t bend down the curve of the 10-year Treasury as much as the Fed had hoped, it still dropped from 4.34% to 3.48%, a total of 86 bps.

Unfortunately, this policy did nothing to remedy the underlying economic problem, which stemmed from a shaky real estate sector. Lending bankers in the overnight repo market had caught on that most, if not all, of the mortgage-backed securities that functioned as loan collateral were loaded with underperforming loans.

By September 2008, the gig was up. Banks in the overnight repo market either refused to buy up bonds collateralized by MBS or would only buy them at exorbitant interest rates, sometimes exceeding 10%.

The sudden withdrawal of liquidity when borrowing banks needed it most resulted in a panic. The overnight repo market seized up, choking off the economy’s oxygen supply and sending the stock market into a dead drop.

Fig. 1

Fig. 2

This dire and unfamiliar catastrophe required a novel solution. That’s when Fed Chair Ben Bernanke stepped in with a controversial remedy.

The Introduction Of Quantitative Easing

"If we acted, nobody would thank us. But if we did not act, who would? Making politically unpopular decisions for the long-run benefit of the country is the reason the Fed exists as a politically independent central bank. It was created for precisely this purpose: to do what must be done—what others cannot or will not do,"

The Courage To Act: A Memoir of a Crisis and Its Aftermath By Ben S. Bernanke

Fed Chair Ben Bernanke had a plan for rescuing the economy. While his measures came with risks, he argued that standing by and doing nothing while the stock market indexes hurtled toward zero was not an option. His solution for the overnight repo market was to quickly inject liquidity into the system by expanding the Federal Reserve’s balance sheet to buy up $1.25 trillion worth of MBS as well as $200 billion worth of corporate bonds. In addition to this, he further drove down rates on long-term Treasuries by reducing the Federal Funds rate to nearly zero. The Fed also purchased $600 billion worth of long-term Treasuries, thereby leveraging the laws of supply and demand. The greater demand for the Treasuries drove up their price, which drove down the interest rates, making borrowing far more affordable for businesses.

Fig. 3

Quantitative Easing quickly turned the stock market around and sent it on a 16-year bull run. In the short run, it was a spectacular success. However, the chart above raises the question of whether the Fed was applying the dubious principle of; if some is good, more is better. In other words, one can only wonder if QE2 and QE3 were necessary or just loaded on for good measure. While QE-4 was understandably enacted to save the economy from the COVID-19 shutdown, it was nonetheless a measure that piled a massive debt load onto both the Federal Reserve Bank and the US Government.

The Quantitative Easing Debt Load

The dramatic rise of the stock market in the aftermath of the Great Recession of 2007-09 coincided with numerous rounds of quantitative easing, as well as a substantial amount of government debt.

As illustrated by the charts below, both our nation’s debt and our debt-to-GDP have soared since the onset of QE in 2008. A return to pre-QE debt-to-GDP levels doesn’t appear to be in the cards.

Fig. 4

Fig. 5

While the Fed made a good faith effort to reduce its balance sheet via quantitative tightening, which it announced in Nov. 2022 and initiated in June 2023, it barely made a dent in the overall amount, as shown in the chart below.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell recently announced that the Fed would reduce its balance sheet run-off from $25 billion to $5 billion per month, one more sign that quantitative easing is a type of economic quicksand that is nearly impossible to escape.

Fig. 6

This gargantuan debt load indicates that the government is incapable of financially recovering from the economic consequences wrought by numerous rounds of QE. Since its enactment, the considerable debt that goes hand-in-hand with QE raises a daunting question.

Does the Fed and the Treasury Dept. have the firepower they need to rescue Wall Street from a steep, long over-due recession with another round of quantitative easing? Or will our fragile financial system buckle under the weight of our increasingly massive debt?

Equity Spikes Are Usually Followed By Equity Crashes

The long-term S&P 500 chart below (Fig. 7) clearly shows the tell-tale spike formation one sees in the lead-up to a market bubble. Especially when you compare the steep trajectory to the S&P 500s run-up preceding the recessions of 2001 and 2008.

Notice the steep incline that occurs after the fourth and final round of quantitative easing. The graph appears to be charting what some might call a melt-up, a phenomenon driven by unreasonable optimism combined with a large number of inexperienced investors.

Fig. 7

We Are Long Overdue For A Recession

The chart below shows that since the mid-1960s, the time between recessions from start-to-start ranges from three to ten years. The time between the beginning of the Great Recession and the beginning of the 2-month COVID-19 flash recession was 12 years, already longer than the longest interval since the 1960s. But disregarding the 2-month mini-recession, the last significant recession was 17 years ago, over twice the average time interval between prior recessions.

Fig. 8

Apparently, since the Great Recession of 2007-09, the Fed has done an excellent job of warding off the natural ebbs and flows of recessionary cycles. But one must question the wisdom of using artificial measures to defy the natural laws of market gravity, especially considering the gravitational force that a record amount of debt could add to the system.

Blinking Red Market Indicators Are Signaling A Recession Is Upon Us

Many market watchers turn to historic charts to look for patterns that have foreshadowed recessionary cycles in the past. The following charts all show formations that indicate we may already be in a recession.

The Inverted Yield Curve

The Inverted Yield Curve is the most reliable indicator for an upcoming recession. It hasn’t been wrong since it was first tracked as an indicator in the 1960s. The latest inversion began in June 2022, just as the Fed began to enact quantitative tightening by allowing US Treasuries and other QE assets to roll off its balance sheet. The 10-year to 2-year Treasury yield re-inversion came two years and two months later in August 2024. This is over twice as long as the second-longest yield curve inversion, which happened from January through December 2000.

Fig. 9

Since two years plus 2 months is by far the longest that a yield curve has been inverted, some investors were lulled into believing that this time around a yield-curve inversion failed to predict a recession and the danger had passed. But once you consider that recessions come only after the re-inversion, we may be experiencing one at this moment. If the length of the most recent yield curve inversion is an indication of the length and depth of the next recession, this upcoming one will likely be significant.

Head and Shoulders Formation Combined With The 50-Day Moving Average

The Dot-com Recession of 2001-02, the Great Recession of 2007-09, and the major correction in 2022 were all preceded by a head-and-shoulders formation that appeared in all the major stock indexes.

When you examine the charts below, you can see that somewhere during the formation of the ‘head’ the index chart line dips below the 50-Day Moving Average line. The 50-Day MA is a technical analysis indicator that averages the closing price of the security over the past 50 days, and this indicates a trend. If the security’s chart line rises above the 50-Day MA, that means it is on an upward trend. If it goes below the 50-Day MA, that means it is on a downward trend.

The head-and-shoulders formation generally indicates that the forward momentum of a stock is running out of steam, and it’s about to go over-the-hill, metaphorically speaking. The bottom of the first ‘armpit’ i.e., the dip just before the head formation, is the ‘neck line’ and this forms the support level. If the chart line’s second armpit dips below the neck line, and then the second shoulder fails to come above the head before dropping again, that’s when the head-and-shoulders formation becomes official since this indicates the stock’s inability to regain its former value—another indication of a downward cycle. When faced with this information, experienced investors must choose between abandoning ship for dry land (i.e., converting stocks to cash and short-term Treasuries) or riding out the storm.

The charts below show the Head & Shoulders formation, as well as the crossing of the 50-Day Moving Average that preceded the Dot-com Recession of 2001-02, the Great Recession of 2007-09, and the correction of 2022.

Fig. 10

Fig. 11

Fig. 12

Shiller PE Ratio

The Shiller PE Ratio, or CAPE ratio, is a valuation metric that allows investors to see if a stock is historically overpriced or underpriced. The values are calculated by dividing the current price of a stock by the average of its earnings over the past 10 years, adjusted for inflation. As you can see in Fig. 13, just recently the Shiller PE Ratio was considerably higher than the lead up to Black Monday of 1929. Another sign that a market bubble may have popped and we are undergoing a steep correction.

Fig. 13

Short-term Treasuries and Hedging With Index Shorts Is A Safe Bet

Roughly sixteen years after the Great Recession of 2007-09, the stock market is signaling that we are on the verge of another major recession. Now is a good time to take profits and go into wealth preservation mode. This would include shifting out of stocks and into short-term Treasuries, anything from one-month to one-year terms. As of April 8, 2025, the current interest rate on 3-month Treasury bills is a respectable 4.30%. Meanwhile, since the beginning of the year, the S&P 500 is down 15.28% while the Nasdaq is down 20.94%.

If you have some select stocks you want to hold on to and ride out the storm, you might consider hedging with an ETF that functions as a put on a major index. ProShares Short S&P500 (SH) rises when the S&P 500 drops, while ProShares Short Dow30 (DOG) rises when the Dow Jones Industrial falls. ProShares Short QQQ (PSQ) rises when the Nasdaq falls.

A short ETF, also known as an inverse ETF, delivers returns that move in the opposite direction of the underlying index, or asset class. So, if you were holding ProShares Short S&P500, if the S&P 500 closed 1% down, then the short ETF would close 1% higher on that same day. ProShares financial managers achieve this result by utilizing investment instruments such as futures contracts, swaps, and options.

All the ProShares ETF derivatives mentioned above return the inverse of their respective indexes on a one-to-one basis.

A Word Of Caution

On Tuesday, April 8, the S&P 500 leapt up by 1.42% (and the Dow 1.55%) on a hopeful rumor that Trump might pause his tariff policy. There is always a chance that the current tariff dispute could be waved away by diplomatic measures and the stock market could resume its upward climb.

On the other hand, Trump is more likely to stick to his guns in regard to bringing back America’s lost manufacturing base. Considering this base was lost over the span of a generation, if Trump insists on a broad-based restoration of US manufacturing in the four years he has left in office, his policies will undoubtedly lead to a great deal of “creative destruction” wherein the stock market would likely be left to fend for itself.

Be that as it may, the Fed has an array of remedies that it can apply to the stock market. A record federal debt might make another large round of QE difficult, but there is a chance that the Fed could once again work its magic to curtail this recession and send the stock market soaring to new highs, as it did in March 2020. It is unlikely the Fed will be able to waive away every recession with another round of QE. But there have been four successful rounds so far, so the Fed will likely attempt a fifth. If QE still works, the people who stay in the stock market will benefit from higher stock prices, while those who go into wealth preservation mode might miss out on those higher gains.

Summing It Up

While President Trump’s tariff policies may be the black swan that has triggered this oncoming recession, the real culprit is four rounds of quantitative easing that left our government, and the Fed, buried in debt. This has set the stage for a long, deep recession that the Fed’s usual remedies probably won't wave away. Taking on ever-soaring debt loads to pay for endless rounds of quantitative easing is unsustainable. Eventually, the system will buckle.

Because quantitative easing coincides with the creation of vast amounts of fiat currency, another round of QE could also stimulate inflation at the height of a recession, thereby triggering the dreaded stagflation—high inflation matched to a high level of unemployment. Leery of such a scenario, institutional bond traders would need larger interest payments to compensate for the risks posed by inflation. That translates into higher interest rates. This in turn would put a further drag on the economy.

The charts in Figures 4, 5, and 6 show a national debt pushed to the limit. This steep debt has already rendered the Fed’s financial remedy of lowering the Federal Funds rate ineffective.

In September 2024, the Fed began lowering the Federal Funds rate, starting off with a 50 bps cut, and following up with two more rate cuts in November and December of 25 bps each, for a total of 100 bps. For over half a century, this method of lowering interest rates across the board has never failed. Until 2024.

Fig. 14

In the final quarter of 2024, as the Fed gradually lowered the Federal Funds rate by 100 basis points, the crucial 10-year Treasury did something unexpected. It rose by an even greater amount, from 3.60% to 4.80%, 120 basis points. Insofar as the 10-year Treasury is the benchmark for home mortgages, car loans, credit cards, small business loans, etc., this could mean that the Fed no longer has the power to stimulate the economy using this method.

How this particular recession pans out is anyone's guess. Tom Barkin, President of the Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond, said it best.

“With all this change, a dense fog has fallen. It's not an everyday, ‘forecasting is hard’ type of fog. It's a ‘zero visibility, pull over and turn on your hazards’ type of fog.”

data.title: StoneCo: This Reversal May Be Just The Beginning
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

I initiated my coverage of StoneCo (NASDAQ:STNE) in mid-August 2023, and while since my very first article, the stock is down ~14%, it's gaining traction lately, being up by almost 37% since mid-January 2025 (my upgrade to "Strong Buy"), outperforming the American broader market by a huge margin:

I think the reversal rally is just getting started because the macro in the STNE's key regions seems to be getting better - the strong unit economics metrics are likely to translate to higher margins in the next quarters. The existing undervaluation given that prospects are setting up STNE stock for another leg higher, in my opinion, so I keep my "Strong Buy" in place today.

Why Do I Think So?

After a period of major stock price decline in recent weeks and months, STNE has been on a steady recovery rally since mid-January 2025, gaining over 41% from the local peak.

In my view, the management rightly characterized 2024 as a "pivotal year of execution." For the full year 2024, StoneCo exceeded its own guidance on several key metrics: retail deposits (BRL 8.7B vs. BRL 7B guidance), MSMB take rate (2.55% vs. 2.49% guidance), credit portfolio size (BRL 1.2B vs. BRL 800M guidance), adjusted administrative expenses (BRL 994M vs. BRL 1.125B guidance), and adjusted net income (BRL 2.2B vs. BRL 1.9B guidance). While the company slightly missed its MSMB card TPV guidance (BRL 403B vs. BRL 412B target), I don't see this as a major concern because their overall operational growth is actually firing on all cylinders:

For Q4 in particular, StoneCo managed to beat the consensus expectations meaningfully - by over 18% and 0.78% on both the bottom and top lines, respectively:

In Q4, the firm's MSMB TPV accelerated to 21% YoY growth while the active MSMB client base also grew by a healthy 19% YoY (up to 4.1 million), so the concerns about fierce competition peak market penetration that I heard before - while staying the key risks for the firm - aren't leading to factual deterioration so far.

Another concerning factor that added to the stock's weakness last year was Brazilian's macro setup - relatively high-interest rates have been a clear major headwind, which has been increasing funding costs. But the management noted during the latest earnings call that they've "implemented substantial repricing across their client base early in Q1 2025 to counteract the higher yield curve", and the latest macro data gives STNE bulls some hope. As reported by Reuters in early April 2025, Brazilian retail sales hit a record high in February, rising 0.5% month-over-month "despite the tight financial conditions." In my understanding, this data point suggests underlying resilience in consumer and business activity, so it's a very positive thing for payment processors like StoneCo, who, as I said above, has already made some steps to solidify its market position. Indirectly, this says that the MSMEs forming StoneCo's core client base are likely finding ways to adapt, potentially sustaining transaction volumes.

I generally like the firm's plan to convert demand deposits into time deposits (CDs) to use for funding operations - the transition that management expects to roll out incrementally through 2025 - because while it logically reduces their float revenue, the lower funding cost should give a net benefit to earnings.

StoneCo's earnings generation is already massive: in Q4 alone, STNE made $665.6 million on an adjusted basis, which is like 20%+ relative to the firm's market cap today. It enables STNE to repurchase its stock from the market en masse. In the past quarter, they bought back BRL 608 million worth of stocks. The current BRL 2 billion buyback program (announced November 2024) had roughly BRL 900 million remaining capacity (as of the earnings call), so extrapolating the current trend further, STNE's share count should soon come back to its 2020 point.

I really liked the management's updated guidance for FY2025 guidance., which assumes an adjusted gross profit of over BRL 7.05B (+14% YoY) and an adjusted Basic EPS of over BRL 8.6 (+18% YoY). So their updated FY2027 targets imply a multi-year growth in TPV at a CAGR of over 14%, a gross profit CAGR of over 18%, and an EPS CAGR of over 27%. The current still-depressed stock price chart presents a disconnect between market sentiment and operational reality, in my view.

As I'm writing these lines, the stock is trading at 6.38x next year's P/E multiple, which is one of the lowest levels in the stock's public history (when matched with the TTM-based price-to-earnings ratio). At the same time, while the goodwill impairment hit the GAAP-based bottom line quite hard, the GP margin is still expanding rapidly, indicating that the firm's business model is working more efficiently than ever before.

Assuming that StoneCo's EPS will be 5-10% higher than currently expected by the consensus for FY2025 - which isn't hard to imagine given the Q4 beat - and also implying a multiple expansion to 10-12x by the year-end, I get a price target of ~$15.9/share. This price target gives me an upside potential of ~38.4%, so I think it's logical to keep my "Strong Buy" unchanged.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

While explaining my bullishness, I may have underestimated the key risks such as the ever-rising competition in the region, the Brazilian macro setup deterioration, and some possible regulatory issues going forward.

As I noted in my previous article on STNE, the interest rate environment in Brazil is pressing STNE's growth hard and makes it quite difficult to keep its NPLs low, so who knows, maybe we'll see a negative surprise on the firm's bottom line during 2025. If my EPS expectations - specifically my input about the 5-10% premium to the current consensus - are off, the stock price is likely to dip further, continuing the 2024 trend.

Given all that, I recommend that everyone reading this article conduct their own due diligence before making any investment decisions.

The Bottom Line

I know that my thesis might be risky for some investors, but from what I see, StoneCo appears to have weathered the worst of the storm. The most important thing is that they're executing well on a new strategy while adapting to market changes like PIX and interest rate hikes. At the same time, on an adjusted basis, STNE's ongoing share repurchase program gives massive support for the stock price and enhances per-share value - as the potential macro reversal is seen by a greater share of market participants, the stock's repricing would be imminent, in my opinion.

The current valuation of STNE stock still seems to offer an attractive entry point for buy-the-dip investors who believe in the continued digitization of Brazil's economy and StoneCo's ability to retain a major share of that market. Based on my calculations, STNE is at least 38.4% undervalued.

I rate STNE a "Strong Buy".

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Pepsi Has A Problem (And A Solution)
data.description:
Stock markets in 2025 have been chaotic and risky. In this environment, my tactic is to hold more "safe" stocks, and I tend to focus on consumer staples, which have been the best-performing sector YTD. PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) is one of the most interesting stocks in this space as it is trading at suppressed prices with an attractive valuation and 3.75% dividend. However, this is usually for a good reason. This article looks at why PEP is struggling and whether PEP can resolve its problems.

PEP Has a Problem

PEP is -5% YTD, even though it is in the best-performing sector.

It's an ideal environment as staples versus discretionary has broken from a long downtrend.

This kind of backdrop usually lifts most stocks in this sector, but not PEP.

PEP's sluggish performance can't be blamed on the sector and it's not due to the soft drinks and non-alcoholic beverages industry either. Coca-Cola (KO) has outperformed significantly since 2023.

PEP sits 11th out of 15 in Seeking Alpha's quant ratings for the industry.

PEP clearly has a stock-specific problem. So what is it, and what can they do about it?

Hiking Prices to Stay Still

The problem is not the price or valuation either. PEP is down around 26% from its 2023 high and trading around the same price as 2020 in the Covid crash. Its forward PE ratio is just 17.46, which is attractive in the current overvalued market. For context, Coca-Cola has a forward PE ratio of over 24.

An interesting chart shows how PEP used to have a higher PE ratio than KO and the valuations were quite correlated. A divergence started in the summer of 2024 and has widened this year.

2024 was the year PEP's revenue growth flatlined and it has not recovered since.

With sales growth hovering around zero, the 17.46 forward PE ratio no longer looks so attractive. Q4 earnings were a disappointment.

The big concern is decreasing volumes in North America across all segments and a decrease in Latin America.

Revenue is practically unchanged, but this is only because PEP keeps raising prices.

TD Cowen analyst Robert Moskow said prices for PepsiCo’s salty snacks, carbonated beverages and sports drinks were up 41% since 2020. The “grocery-store average,” he said, was 25%.

This isn't a sustainable path for the company, especially since consumer spending is coming under pressure in 2025. Last week's consumer sentiment survey index dropped to near-record lows as tariff/inflation concerns weighed. Besides, the price increases are clearly hurting volumes and consumers may be shifting to other cheaper brands.

I would not mind flat revenues if volumes were increasing as it would at least show demand for products. Prices could be hiked later. The way things are now is not healthy and the market knows it.

The Positives

PEP is a dividend aristocrat and just raised its dividend 5% in its last earnings:

The Company today announced a 5 percent increase in its annualized dividend to $5.69 per share from $5.42 per share, effective with the dividend expected to be paid in June 2025. This represents the Company’s 53rd consecutive annual dividend per share increase.

Its current 3.75% yield is the best it's ever been.

While its earnings were disappointing, flat revenues are better than falling revenues. PEP should not be in any danger and dividend increases are likely to continue.

Furthermore, some of the issues can be addressed. One problem is that many of PEP's brands are seen as unhealthy, and consumer trends are shifting to healthier products. PEP is clearly aware of this and in March '25 it acquired Poppi, a "fast-growing prebiotic soda brand."

CEO Ramon Laguarta talked about some healthier options in Frito during the Q4 conference call:

...we're moving more of our A&M dollars towards, what we call, positive choices or permissible offerings for the consumer, a new line of no artificials under Simply, which will have all our brands, more effort on baked, more effort on lightly salted, more efforts on parts of the portfolio where we see consumers moving, a lot of effort on portion control, a lot of effort on single serve, on multi-packs and a lot of efforts on availability of our small portions.

Another problem is that the price increases have made PEP products seem like a poor value compared to other products. Again, PEP has started to address this, with Laguarta saying this in the conference call:

I think when we talk to consumers, value is the number one decision maker and it's the reason why the category slowed down in the last 12 months. So, we think that addressing value, given the consumers' choices at different price points, different solutions throughout the month, the consumers will be making choices as they try and to maximize their disposable income. So, I think that continues to be the number one focus and I think we have a much stronger pricing, sizing and promotional activities that address that with high ROI and maximizing the value of the category.

Addressing value doesn't necessarily mean price cuts. He continued,

No, I wouldn't assume that we're going to have negative pricing. I don't think that's our strategy. What I'm saying is we're going to have much more surgical offerings to consumers, especially around price partitions, which I think we can do price and sizing in a way that we give consumers optionality without diluting the pricing of our business or the category.

I like that the company is addressing this issue, and if they can offer value without cutting prices, all the better. Whether it works is another matter and I doubt it will add enough demand to make up for not hiking prices. The risk, then, is that revenues decrease.

What I would like to see in Q1 earnings on April 24, is volumes stabilizing (no more drop-off in North America) and for revenues to stabilize without price hikes. That would be encouraging for low single-digit growth and a recovery in the share price.

Conclusions

PEP has underperformed its sector and its peers. The market has punished its lack of revenue growth, even when prices have increased.

The company has recognized the two main problems limiting its growth: a shift to healthier products and a lack of value for the consumer. It is addressing these issues and I am hoping to see stable revenues without price increases. This should stabilize the share price and make the 3.75% dividend yield attractive. I currently rate the stock a "hold."

data.title: April 2025 Commentary And Economic Outlook
data.description:
Top Headlines from Commentary and Economic Outlook

  • Headline CPI was negative at .1%, and core at positive .1%, with Y/Y headline dropping to 2.4% and core to 2.8%. The cool print was driven by plunging oil prices and other volatile components, including lodging, airline fares, and used cars. Shelter continued to be distorted, with owners' equivalent rent rising to .4% from .3% despite market rents continuing to rise only 1% per year.

  • We continue to be bullish on bonds with a 3.75% year-end yield target on the 10-year. The economy is slowing, and weakness in the US job market will likely force the Fed to cut three times this year. The market had failed to recognize that tariffs are recessionary/deflationary, as the tax revenue reduces the deficit and the price increases are one-time and should be ignored by the Fed. We believe the latest sell-off in Treasuries was technical and will return to normal over time.

  • We expect earnings season will be a positive catalyst for the market as companies provide details on how they will cope with tariff increases, although investment banks are likely to miss earnings estimates due to market turmoil. Tax day could be a negative catalyst, however, as investors fund tax payments with stock sales.

  • The market is ignoring the impact on inflation of a 14% plunge in the price of energy. There is a 5% bleed through of energy prices to core inflation which could offset all or a part of the increase in import prices. Headline inflation could be negative over the next few months as lower energy and refined products prices feed through to inflation indices.

  • Energy is 6% of headline inflation (and 5% of PPI), so a 14% reduction in energy prices will trim CPI by almost 1% and will reduce core by up to 1% as lower energy prices bleed through to core slowly over time.

Economics & Fed

  • We do not expect a US recession, as the economy is supported by the fact that the bond market has cut long-term rates for the Fed. Oil prices have dropped 14% this year, and tech spending is likely to remain strong. Additionally, there is a pending $1.5tn tax cut currently being passed by Congress.

  • Our estimate of PCE based on CPI is only .1%. Energy prices continued to plunge after the measurement period of March CPI, which means April headline inflation is likely to also print cool. CPI-R, our real time estimate of CPI, is only up 1.1% year-over-year. We continued to believe that the Fed is making a serious policy error by not cutting rates, as inflation is clearly dropping rapidly and already below the Fed’s target if the 2-year lagged shelter estimate is corrected to use real-time pricing of rents.

  • Price increases from tariffs should be treated as a one-time increase in prices and be ignored for the purpose of formulating monetary policy. The effect of new tariffs will be out of the inflation data after the next two months, which should allow the Fed to see that inflation is actually dropping due to ultra-tight monetary policy and plunging oil prices. We continue to believe that the Fed will cut 3 times this year and that the 10-year yield will end the year in the 3.5-4.0% range after the Fed cuts rates.

  • The US economic growth is decelerating rapidly, with growth likely to drop from over 3% into the 1-2% range as the effects of the Fed’s ultra-tight monetary policy impacts the residential and commercial construction industries and the deflationary/recessionary impacts of Trump Administration tariffs and DOGE layoffs impact the economy.

Stock Market

  • We are neutral-to-negative on the stock market and have pulled our price targets pending more clarity on tariff war, tax bill and Fed policy.

  • Corporate tax cuts are the key driver of economic growth and stock prices. The IMF estimated that a 10% increase in country savings drives economic growth by 1.5%.

  • We expect the market to stabilize in mid-April after we get greater clarity on tariffs and income taxes and we enter earnings season. There will be substantial support for the stock market in the 5,000 area for the S&P.

  • The US is experiencing StagDeflation as growth decelerates and inflation continues to decline well below the Fed’s arbitrary 2% target. Tariff increases are one-time increases (as the Fed Chair acknowledged) and should be excluded from core inflation.

  • The Trump administration has made a policy error by leading with tariff tax increases well before we have visibility on the tax cut bill. This policy error has created uncertainty about the US economy and destabilized the stock market. The tariff policy has also frozen the Fed, which should be cutting rates now.

Bond Market

  • We continue to be bullish on bonds with a 3.75% year-end yield target on the 10-year. The market has failed to recognize that the economy is slowing (see details below) and weakness in the US job market will likely force the Fed to cut three times this year. The market had failed to recognize that tariffs are recessionary/deflationary, as the tax revenue reduces the deficit and the price increases are one-time and should be ignored by the Fed.

  • Changes in 10-year Treasury inflation breakevens (ILBE on terminal) dropped by over .2% to 2.2% since the “Chart of Death” was unveiled by President Trump at the “Liberation Day” press conference.

  • Inflation is always caused by excessive money supply growth as occurred during the Pandemic (22% inflation with 22% excess money supply growth), and never by tariffs and deportation. The money supply (M0) shrank 5% Y/Y, indicating that prices will continue to decline.

  • The “Hatfield Rule” is a recession indicator which states that if housing starts drop below 1.1MM, there will be a recession. It is superior to the “Sahm” rule, as housing is a leading indicator and employment is a lagging indicator.

Commodities

  • We are lowering our 2025 target on oil from $80 to $70 (range of $60-80), as it has become clear that Trump will use his influence with the Saudis and Russia to limit price increases despite tighter sanction on Iran. This policy will offset a good portion of one-time price increases from tariffs.

  • Pollution taxes are by far the most economic method to rapidly reduce carbon and improve the environment. Limiting natural gas production is highly destructive to the global environment and has led to regime change in Europe.

  • President Trump has indicated that he will pressure OPEC, particularly Saudi Arabia, to increase oil production and keep prices low. At the same time, Trump supports domestic drilling, which is positive for U.S. production volumes. Therefore, companies with volume exposure have outperformed those with commodity price sensitivity. We have lowered our oil price target to $60-70 per barrel.

  • Artificial Intelligence and data centers have opened up new growth prospects for natural gas midstream companies to supply gas-fired power plants. Natural gas plants have some of the shortest times to build, and we believe they are best-positioned to supply reliable power quickly.
